ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken Pepper Fry (Kozhi Milagu Varuval). Mapped to the closest parent 'chicken dish' class (FOODON_03301116) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chettinad-style dry pepper chicken. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is used as the closest parent FoodEx2 anchor; spice coating and dry-fry preparation are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for Chicken Pepper Fry as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chicken Pepper Fry is approximately 200 g, representing a standard side-dish portion alongside rice and rasam in a South Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN) / ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Chicken Pepper Fry are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using cooked chicken (bone-in, spiced) as the closest approved reference. Values for spice-derived micronutrients (manganese, selenium) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for black pepper and cumin. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values originally sourced from a delivery platform have been replaced with ICMR-NIN / USDA-derived estimates. All per_100g values reflect the cooked, spice-coated preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate for high-protein dry-spiced chicken preparation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Pepper Fry (Kozhi Milagu Varuval). GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 9.0) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3. The dish's negligible net carbohydrate content (predominantly from spices and shallots) supports a low GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) and added salt in preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced salt during preparation, as a 200 g serving contributes approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 42% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and high protein content (~18.5 g/100 g) supporting postprandial glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Pepper Fry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content; a 200 g serving contributes only ~9 g carbohydrate, promoting satiety and stable blood glucose, though pairing with large portions of white rice should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g) from chicken; partially offset by piperine and anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Chicken Pepper Fry in moderate portions (100–150 g), prepared with minimal oil and reduced salt, as the dish provides beneficial lean protein and anti-inflammatory compounds from black pepper and curry leaves while keeping saturated fat within acceptable limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Piperine in black pepper enhances curcumin absorption; anti-inflammatory spice profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The piperine content of black pepper in this dish significantly enhances the bioavailability of anti-inflammatory curcumin from turmeric; however, individuals with gastric sensitivity should moderate intake due to the high spice level.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Pepper Fry, known in Tamil as Kozhi Milagu Varuval, is a signature dry-fried chicken dish from the Chettinad region of Tamil Nadu, South India. It is prepared by slow-roasting small bone-in chicken pieces in a high-heat dry sauté (varuval) technique with a coarse spice paste of crushed black peppercorns, fennel seeds, and cumin, along with shallots, curry leaves, ginger, garlic, and turmeric. The dish is cooked until all moisture evaporates, producing a dark, intensely aromatic, and deeply spiced coating on the chicken. The dominant heat comes from black pepper rather than red chillies, giving it a warming, pungent character distinct from other South Indian chicken preparations. Texturally, the exterior is dry and slightly crisp while the chicken remains tender inside. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 185 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and only 4.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a low glycemic index of approximately 25. It is rich in niacin (B3), vitamin B6, phosphorus, and zinc from the chicken, and contributes bioactive compounds including piperine from black pepper, which has demonstrated anti-inflammatory and bioavailability-enhancing properties in peer-reviewed literature. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein satiety effect, and is appropriate for cardiovascular health when prepared with minimal oil. Sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. It is served as a side dish with rice and rasam, or as an appetizer, and is particularly popular at lunch and dinner in South Indian households and restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chicken Pepper Fry?
Is Chicken Pepper Fry good for diabetes?
What spices are in Chettinad Chicken Pepper Fry?
Is Chicken Pepper Fry high in protein?
What is the difference between Chicken Pepper Fry and Pepper Chicken Masala?
How is Varuval different from curry?
Can I eat Chicken Pepper Fry if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: dinner accompaniment with rice and rasam

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using the closest approved cooked chicken reference, supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for spice-derived micronutrients. No exact match for Chettinad-style Chicken Pepper Fry exists in any single approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values revised from input: the original input carbohydrate value of 21.25 g/100 g was inconsistent with the dish description (dry-fried chicken with spices and shallots); revised to 4.5 g/100 g based on ICMR-NIN cooked chicken reference and spice/shallot contribution. Energy recalculated accordingly. 2. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values originally attributed to 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery-platform source) have been replaced with ICMR-NIN / USDA-derived estimates. 3. Portions section in input contained an erroneous entry ('1 medium pizza, approx. 8 inches, 6 slices, 400 g') which is clearly a data-entry error for this dish; this entry has been excluded. Duplicate '1 serving (200 g)' entries consolidated to a single 200 g default serving. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to 4.2 and 2.1 respectively based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fatty acid profile for cooked chicken thigh/drumstick with skin. 5. water_g set to 62.0 based on ICMR-NIN cooked chicken reference. 6. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (25 × 9.0 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 2.3. 7. This is a South Indian / Chettinad dish, not a Singaporean dish; Chinese and Malay multilingual fields are not mandatory and are set to null. Tamil and Hindi local names are provided as the dish originates from Tamil Nadu and is widely known across India. Malayalam and Telugu aliases are included in aliases_by_language as supplementary regional coverage. 8. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type, conforming to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. The previous iteration used 'broader' which was not a valid enumeration value.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON; forbidden source (grabfood) values replaced; erroneous pizza portion entry removed; carbohydrate revised from 21.25 g to 4.5 g to match dish profile; energy recalculated.
Iteration 2: Ontology match types set to 'broader' — subsequently identified as invalid enumeration value by validator.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. G5 hard gate now passes.
